fields.description:
## Context
Engineers running fix-bug, review resolution, or sprint implementation agents experience wasted retry cycles when agents encounter unfamiliar tools, complex shell patterns, or external integration behaviors. Agents exhibit "confident ignorance" — generating plausible but wrong fixes from training data rather than searching for proven implementations. Each failed retry compounds the problem into cascading bugs. In recent sessions (e.g., ACLI Jira priority, bash script syntax), engineers had to manually redirect agents to search GitHub for how other projects solved the same problem. Today, WebSearch is only used in fix-bug's escalated investigation agent for error pattern matching — not for researching prior art. Resolution sub-agents and sprint implementation agents have no prior-art search capability at all.

## Success Criteria
1. A shared prompt fragment in plugins/dso/skills/shared/ defines bright-line triggers as an enumerable checklist for when agents must search for prior art before writing or modifying code. Verified by a promptfoo eval with >=3 boundary scenarios confirming consistent search/no-search classification across independent agent runs.
2. Agents check project documentation and codebase patterns first (cheap tier), with a trust validation gate: open bugs related to a pattern are a hard blocker; recent CI failures on the same files are a blocker; recent git history of fixes to the same pattern reduces trust; passing tests and consistent usage are soft confidence signals only. Decision rule: if any hard blocker is present, the pattern is untrusted regardless of soft signals and proceeds to the tiered search in SC3; if no hard blockers and at least one soft confidence signal, the pattern is trusted.
3. When project patterns are absent or untrusted, agents execute a narrow GitHub code search (budget: ~6 calls) for working implementations of the specific tool/pattern, then a broader outcome-reframed search (budget: ~10 calls) targeting the desired outcome if narrow fails, then escalate to user. In non-interactive contexts (sprint sub-agents), escalation produces a structured result indicating the agent cannot proceed, rather than blocking.
4. fix-bug (SKILL.md Step 6 code-modification gate), the code review resolution sub-agent (docs/workflows/prompts/review-fix-dispatch.md), and the sprint implementation agent (sprint SKILL.md Phase 5 sub-agents) all reference the shared prompt fragment at their respective decision points.
5. The bright-line triggers explicitly exclude routine modifications: single-file logic changes within existing patterns, test reversions, and formatting/lint fixes. Verified by eval scenarios confirming no false-positive triggers on these categories.
6. Applied to the 3 most recent confident-ignorance incidents from project history (e.g., ACLI priority gap, bash syntax cascading failures), agents either find a working prior-art reference or correctly escalate — with zero confident-wrong-fix retry cycles.

## Scenario Analysis
- GitHub code search budget exhausted by noisy queries (critical): Poorly scoped queries exhaust the narrow-search budget with irrelevant results. Non-interactive contexts need a fallback that produces a structured escalation result rather than blocking indefinitely.
- Bright-line trigger mismatch: agent evaluates soft signals before hard blockers (high): A pattern with consistent codebase usage (soft signal) but an open bug (hard blocker) creates contradictory signals. The prompt must enforce hard-blocker evaluation before soft signals to prevent silent bypass.
- Resolution sub-agent lacks WebSearch/WebFetch access (high): Resolution sub-agents in sub-agent context may not have web search tools. The framework needs a fallback path — escalate to orchestrator for tool-enabled retry or return a structured cannot-proceed result.
- New consuming skill added without fragment reference (medium): No automated check verifies new skills include the shared fragment. Coverage degrades silently.
- Fragment path breaks on directory restructure (high): If shared/prompts/ is reorganized, consuming skill references silently break — the agent proceeds without the framework entirely.

## Dependencies
None. Related but distinct:
- dafa-b1fe (hallucination reduction in exploration via CoVe — different trigger point)
- ab57-b534 (spike verification during implementation planning — different phase)
- 2e8e-72d9 (splitting large search operations into sub-tasks — different mechanism)

## Approach
Create a shared markdown prompt fragment in plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/ defining bright-line triggers, the tiered search protocol (project docs -> trust check -> narrow GitHub search -> broad outcome search -> user escalation), and trust validation rules. Each consuming skill includes this fragment at its key code-modification decision point. No new scripts; the decision logic lives entirely in prompt guidance.
